sound_type: shack-shack-shack raucous alarm call -- harsh, penetrating, staccato sentinel voice of the conifer forest
ecosystem: Conifer forest -- Douglas fir, western red cedar, western hemlock, Sitka spruce. Dominant corvid of the conifer canopy.
season: Year-round resident. Alarm calls given throughout the year whenever threats are detected.

coordinates.description: Reference for conifer forest habitat in the PNW, Washington. Steller's Jays found throughout conifer forests from sea level to timberline across the Pacific Northwest.

taxonomy_note.description: RETURN SPECIES -- Steller's Jay first appeared at degree 53 (FIRST CORVID). Now returns in raucous-alarm context at degree 103. Same family (Corvidae), same species, different acoustic. 50 degrees between entries. FOURTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-103): Raven, Raven pair, American Crow, Steller's Jay.
taxonomy_note.family_count_in_engine: Corvidae count: 5 (Steller's Jay 53, Raven 100, Raven pair 101, American Crow 102, Steller's Jay raucous 103)
taxonomy_note.name_etymology: Cyanocitta from Greek kyanos ('blue') and kitta ('jay'). stelleri after Georg Wilhelm Steller, the German naturalist who first described the species during Vitus Bering's 1741 expedition to Alaska. Common name 'Steller's Jay' honors the same naturalist.
taxonomy_note.genus_note: Cyanocitta -- the blue jays. Two species: Steller's Jay (western) and Blue Jay (eastern). Steller's Jay is the western counterpart.
taxonomy_note.order_note: Passeriformes. Fourth consecutive corvid passerine at degrees 100-103.
taxonomy_note.family_position: Corvidae entered at degree 100 (Common Raven). The Steller's Jay was the FIRST CORVID at degree 53, predating Corvidae's formal family entry. Now returns within the Corvidae quartet.

song_metrics.song_frequency_hz: Alarm call: harsh broadband burst centered around 2,500-4,000 Hz. Not tonal -- noisy, rasping quality.
song_metrics.song_duration_s: Each 'shack' note: 0.1-0.2 seconds. Series of 3-8 notes in rapid succession.
song_metrics.note_structure: STACCATO HARSH BURST: Each alarm note is a short, sharp, harsh burst of broadband noise. Rapidly repeated in series. No melodic content -- pure alarm signal. Penetrating and attention-grabbing.
song_metrics.note_types: Alarm call (shack-shack-shack), territorial scold, hawk mimicry, soft whisper song (rare), various mimicked calls. Degree 103 focuses specifically on the raucous alarm -- the loudest, most penetrating vocalization.
song_metrics.phrase_structure: Alarm series: 3-8 harsh notes in rapid succession, often repeated after brief pause. Intensity and rate increase with threat proximity. No melodic phrase structure -- signal-based.
song_metrics.timbre: Harsh. Rasping. Metallic. Penetrating. The antithesis of melodic. Designed to startle and alert. The most abrasive timbre in the Corvidae quartet -- contrasts with the raven's resonant cronk and the crow's cleaner caw.
song_metrics.amplitude: E=5 -- HIGH energy. The alarm call is one of the loudest sounds in the conifer forest. Projects across hundreds of meters of dense canopy. Designed for maximum audibility in the forest's acoustically challenging environment.
song_metrics.tempo: Rapid staccato. 4-8 notes per second during intense alarm. Tempo increases with threat proximity. The fastest repeated vocalization in the Corvidae quartet.
song_metrics.context: ALARM AND SENTINEL. Given when detecting aerial predators (hawks, owls), terrestrial predators (cats, weasels), and human intruders. Also used in territorial defense and mobbing behavior. The primary function is community-level warning -- other species (squirrels, small birds, deer) respond to the jay's alarm as a reliable predator alert.
song_metrics.regional_variation: Moderate. Some geographic variation in alarm call structure. Cultural learning documented. Mimicry of Red-tailed Hawk calls is widespread -- the jay uses hawk mimicry as a deceptive alarm to scatter competitors from food sources.
song_metrics.key_property: SHACK-SHACK-SHACK RAUCOUS ALARM -- the sentinel voice of the conifer forest. Harsh, penetrating, staccato. E=5. Community-level warning broadcast. RETURN SPECIES from degree 53 (FIRST CORVID). FOURTH CONSECUTIVE CORVIDAE (100-103). Different acoustic from degree 53 -- raucous alarm vs mimicry/variety. The loudest voice in the Corvidae quartet. Forty-third consecutive original CSV.

plumage.overall: Large crested jay. Deep blue body, black head and upper breast, prominent crest. No white markings (unlike Blue Jay).
plumage.diagnostic_mark: For degree 103: (1) TALL BLACK CREST -- the most prominent crest of any PNW bird. (2) DEEP BLUE body. (3) BLACK head and upper breast. (4) NO WHITE -- distinguishes from Blue Jay (which has white wing bars and tail corners).
plumage.seasonal_variation: Minimal. Year-round plumage. Crest can be raised or lowered depending on mood and context -- fully raised during alarm and aggression.
plumage.flight_pattern: Undulating flight with rapid wingbeats. Often flies from tree to tree through the canopy rather than across open spaces.

physical_description.overall: Large crested jay. Body length 30-34 cm. Wingspan 43 cm. Weight 100-140 g. Deep blue body, black head and upper breast, prominent black crest.
physical_description.diagnostic_mark: For degree 103 (raucous context): crest fully raised during alarm calls -- the visual signal accompanies the acoustic signal. The raised crest + harsh alarm is the jay's complete warning display.
physical_description.field_id: Large crested jay in conifer forest. Deep blue body, black head, tall black crest. Harsh shack-shack-shack alarm call. If you hear a loud, harsh, staccato alarm in a conifer forest -- it is almost certainly a Steller's Jay.

nodes.title: The Raucous Alarm -- shack-shack-shack -- Sentinel Voice of the Conifer Forest
nodes.description: Harsh, penetrating, staccato alarm call. E=5. Projects across hundreds of meters of dense conifer canopy. Community-level warning broadcast -- other species respond to the jay's alarm as reliable predator alert. Multi-level warning protocol: different patterns for different threat types. The loudest voice in the Corvidae quartet.

nodes.title: Fourth Consecutive Corvidae (100-103) -- The Corvid Quartet
nodes.description: Raven (100, deep cronk), Raven pair (101, duet), American Crow (102, sharp caw), Steller's Jay (103, raucous alarm). Four degrees, one family, four different acoustic characters. The FIRST CORVID (53) returns to close the quartet. Four consecutive Corvidae is the longest same-family run in the bird section of the engine.

nodes.title: Return Species -- Degree 53 to Degree 103 (50 Degrees Apart)
nodes.description: Steller's Jay first appeared at degree 53 -- the FIRST CORVID in the engine. That entry centered on mimicry and vocal variety. Degree 103 reframes in raucous-alarm context -- the harsh, penetrating alarm call. Same species, different acoustic center. 50 degrees between entries. Like the raven pair (100-101) and other species pairs, the Jay gets two entries for two fundamentally different vocalizations.

nodes.title: Conifer Forest Sentinel -- Community-Level Warning
nodes.description: The Steller's Jay is the sentinel species of PNW conifer forests. Its alarm calls structure the forest's collective awareness of danger. Other species -- Douglas Squirrel (80), small passerines, deer -- respond to jay alarms as reliable predator warnings. The jay's sentinel role makes it a keystone acoustic species -- its voice organizes the forest's collective response to threat.

nodes.title: Douglas Squirrel (80) -- Conifer Canopy Neighbor
nodes.description: Two vocal, territorial conifer-canopy species. The squirrel's territorial chatter and the jay's alarm call are two of the most common sounds in PNW conifer forests. Both serve warning functions. Both project through dense canopy. Acoustic companions in the same vertical habitat.

cross_references.relationship: SAME SPECIES, DIFFERENT ACOUSTIC! Degree 53 centered on mimicry and vocal variety -- the FIRST CORVID. Degree 103 centers on the raucous shack-shack-shack alarm call. 50 degrees between entries. Same species, two voices in the engine.

CORVIDAE QUARTET (100-103). Raven starts the quartet with deep cronk. Jay closes the quartet with raucous alarm. Both Corvidae, maximally different acoustic characters -- deep resonant voice vs harsh staccato alarm.

CORVIDAE QUARTET (100-103). Crow in open/urban habitat, Jay in conifer forest. Complementary niches. Crow's sharp caw vs Jay's harsh shack-shack-shack. Different habitats, different alarm architectures.
CORVIDAE QUARTET (100-103). Raven pair duet vocalizations vs Jay alarm call. Pair-bonding context vs sentinel context. Two different social functions within one family.
CONIFER CANOPY NEIGHBORS. Both vocal, territorial, conifer-canopy species. The squirrel's territorial chatter and the jay's alarm call are the two most common sounds in PNW conifer forests.
